module.name: output_intent
module.spec: Drift's output has three distinct consumer types, each with fundamentally different needs: interactive humans at a terminal (need legible, scannable output with visual cues), humans piping output to other tools or files (need clean text without escape codes), and software consuming drift programmatically (need structured data, not prose to regex-parse). A single output format cannot serve all three simultaneously. See the dual audience constraint.

When a human runs drift in a terminal, the default experience must be immediately legible — drift, status, and structure must be visible at a glance without reading prose. Color is the primary tool for this. The default output mode is Color when stdout is a terminal (see color default), not Plain. See the developer persona.

Software consuming drift — particularly LLM coding agents — must be able to parse output programmatically without regex-parsing human-formatted text. JSON is the contract for this. Every command must produce a defined JSON shape so an agent can rely on field names, types, and structure. See the LLM agent persona.

When drift output is piped, redirected, or consumed by another process, color codes MUST NOT appear in the output. ANSI escape sequences corrupt pipelines, files, and structured parsers. This is non-negotiable: pipe-safety is enforced by automatic TTY detection, not by user opt-in. A user should never have to remember --no-color when running `drift todo | grep marker`. See TTY detection and flag precedence. See also the CI persona.

Users must be able to override drift's automatic mode selection. --no-color forces Plain; --json forces JSON; --color=always forces Color even when not a TTY. These overrides respect the precedence rules in flag precedence.

Every command's output in every mode is a defined contract. There are no "best effort" or "implementation-defined" outputs. Plain, Color, and JSON outputs are all specified; the cross-mode equivalence (Color modulo ANSI == Plain) is an enforced invariant, not a coincidence. See the guardrail property.

Errors are first-class output, not afterthoughts. Every error path produces an ErrorResult with a defined shape in every mode. There are no bare-string error returns anywhere in the command pipeline. See the error result invariant.

Command metadata (name, usage, recognized flags) must live in exactly one place. Three sources of truth (help.txt, subcommandHelpTexts, recognizedFlags) was three opportunities to drift. The Registry collapses them: every command declares its own metadata; help text and flag validation are derived. See the Registry.

Every command flows through the same dispatch pipeline. The pre-dispatch uniform checks (help_flag, unknown_flag_rejection, global_flag_stripping) and the post-dispatch uniform handling (presenter.Render, exit-code propagation) apply identically to all commands including version. No command bypasses the pipeline. See the dispatch pipeline.
The output layer adds no external dependencies. ANSI codes, TTY detection, JSON serialization all use Go stdlib only. This protects drift's zero-dependency commitment. See zero dependency portable.
The output layer is itself drift-tracked. Every new spec has a marker in the implementing code; every behavior change updates a spec first; drift todo is clean before every commit. See self hosting.

Drift's visual identity is configurable, not hardcoded. Different users have different terminals (dark vs light backgrounds, specific color schemes), different aesthetic preferences (minimal vs vibrant), and different accessibility needs (color blindness). A theming system with 14 independently-configurable elements lets each user get output that works for them without modifying drift's source code. Themes are selectable at runtime via --theme=NAME or fully overridden via .drift/theme.xml. The guardrail property (guardrail) holds for every theme. See the dual audience constraint and the theme system.
